Nebraska is such an incredibly powerful album and has remained my favorite album from The Boss. It’s honestly one of the most personal pieces of music I have ever heard and the book this film is based on captured the story of making that album so well. I cannot wait for this.

In 1996 I bought a used copy from a Record store/Pawn shop in Clintonville, Ohio. (tiny shop near Nacy’s restaurant on High St) Bruce said the songs are like “Prayers”. I didn’t know he said that until years later.  My daughter and I would hit these  secondhand shops on High Street after she had enough of the school playground. Needed to get out of the stuffy apartment at least once every day. My wife was better at reading stories or doing art with her while I was at school. I was better at taking our daughter for walks or grocery shopping while my wife was at school. We always had trouble sleeping especially being a young parent in a strange town. I stumbled on the Nebraska album at the right time. Vinyl was not popular so we could afford it.  It was a record of “Brand New Used”  songs just for us. Beautiful  , sad , hopeful and timeless songs. I love when a song on this album becames an “ear worm” randomly stuck on repeat in my head. Effortlessly surrounded by love and hope. Surrounded by prayers. A beautiful place to be

Honestly, I was really nervous about this movie from the time it was announced, but this trailer puts me at rest. Bruce is a magical artist, both in writing and performance. And that magic is rooted in how he captures the existential joys and torments of life itself. I was worried that this movie would be too Hollywood to really capture this chapter in his work. If the songs and sound of the electric Nebraska had been his next entry rather than Born in the U.S.A, that would have taken his career in an entirely different direction, which honestly could have impacted how American culture developed.

@drom93 I'm actually with you on this. I don't see the appeal of these rock hagiographies-- the one about Queen either. They're exercises in mimicry that serves the interests of Hollywood; much more about the career of the actors in them than the musicians they're supposedly about.
​@robertoclemente5512What part of those movies are about the career of the actors in them. Not one day passes on yt comments without someone throwing some complete nonsense out there.

@meciocio What part? The movies, in their entirety, are vehicles to promote these stars: Rami Malek, Timothée Chalamet, and now JAW. It's a Hollywood "small movie" formula akin to "putting on the cape" for mindless superhero trash; stars do it for Oscar bids. 

Rock bio-pics simplify and dumb down the contributions of the artists they historicize. Freddie Mercury's queerness is defanged. Dylan's complex involvement with politics is reduced to "acoustic or electric"-- the choice to privilege his individuality over a collective movement (allegory of capitalism). "The Boss," I'm guessing, will be lionized for leaving his blue-collar world behind while milking it for blockbuster singlez.

​​​​​@robertoclemente5512I want to understand how is the Bohemian Rhapsody movie about the career of the actors in it and not literally about the historical figures and events they try to portray?

If you think the movies are bad instead of good, that's a complete different story. But don't come at me with this "the movies are not about the characters they portray but about the careers of the actors playing thrm" poser edgy nonsense
